mainEntity.name: Why does my son is smart academically but completely lost socially. he eats lunch alone every day. he doesn't know how to join a game at recess — he either stands at the edge watching or barges in and the kids get annoyed. when someone says 'what's up?' he starts explaining what's literally above him. he takes everything literally. he can't tell when kids are joking, when they're being mean, or when they're trying to be friendly. he interrupts constantly because he doesn't understand conversation flow. he talks about his special interest to anyone who will listen — or won't listen — and doesn't notice when they're trying to escape. his teachers say he's 'socially immature' but i don't know how to teach him things that other kids just seem to absorb naturally. he comes home crying because he has no friends. he tries so hard but keeps getting it wrong. the other kids have started avoiding him. i've tried explaining social situations but it doesn't stick. it's like he needs to see it, practice it, have a script for it. group projects are a disaster because he either tries to control everything or withdraws completely. he doesn't understand the unwritten rules — like you don't tell someone their drawing is bad even if it's true, or you can't just sit at any lunch table. he misses sarcasm completely. he can't read facial expressions well enough to know when someone is annoyed with him. by the time he realizes something went wrong socially, the damage is already done and he has no idea how to repair it. i feel like i'm watching him drown in a sea of social rules that everyone else can swim through effortlessly. the school says he needs 'social skills support' but what does that actually look like? what materials actually help? how do i teach him skills that most kids learn without being taught? because every day he comes home more isolated, more confused, and more convinced that something is wrong with him?

acceptedAnswer.text: SOCIAL COMMUNICATION DIFFICULTIES / SOCIAL PRAGMATIC COMMUNICATION DISORDER / SOCIAL SKILLS DEFICITS / PEER RELATIONSHIP DIFFICULTIES / SOCIAL COGNITION CHALLENGES! Social skills at school encompass the complex set of abilities required to successfully navigate peer interactions, classroom dynamics, and the unwritten social rules of educational settings. These skills include: initiating and maintaining conversations, joining ongoing activities appropriately, taking turns, cooperating in groups, reading social cues (facial expressions, body language, tone of voice), understanding perspective of others, recognizing and responding to emotions, using appropriate humor, resolving conflicts, and following the implicit social hierarchy and rules of the school environment. For neurotypical children, many of these skills develop naturally through observation and implicit learning. For children with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, social communication disorder, anxiety, or other conditions, these skills often need to be explicitly taught, practiced, and supported with visual aids and structured approaches. School presents unique social challenges: the playground is unstructured and fast-moving, the cafeteria requires navigating seating politics, group work demands collaboration skills, and classroom participation requires understanding when and how to speak. Children who struggle socially at school often experience isolation, bullying, anxiety, and declining self-esteem — even when their academic performance is strong. Social skills intervention focuses on making the invisible rules visible, providing practice opportunities, and building the cognitive frameworks for understanding social situations.

mainEntity.name: What is Social Communication Difficulties / Social Pragmatic Communication Disorder / Social Skills Deficits / Peer Relationship Difficulties / Social Cognition Challenges?

acceptedAnswer.text: Social skills at school encompass the complex set of abilities required to successfully navigate peer interactions, classroom dynamics, and the unwritten social rules of educational settings. These skills include: initiating and maintaining conversations, joining ongoing activities appropriately, taking turns, cooperating in groups, reading social cues (facial expressions, body language, tone of voice), understanding perspective of others, recognizing and responding to emotions, using appropriate humor, resolving conflicts, and following the implicit social hierarchy and rules of the school environment. For neurotypical children, many of these skills develop naturally through observation and implicit learning. For children with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, social communication disorder, anxiety, or other conditions, these skills often need to be explicitly taught, practiced, and supported with visual aids and structured approaches. School presents unique social challenges: the playground is unstructured and fast-moving, the cafeteria requires navigating seating politics, group work demands collaboration skills, and classroom participation requires understanding when and how to speak. Children who struggle socially at school often experience isolation, bullying, anxiety, and declining self-esteem — even when their academic performance is strong. Social skills intervention focuses on making the invisible rules visible, providing practice opportunities, and building the cognitive frameworks for understanding social situations. Common signs include: Has difficulty making or keeping friends at school, Eats lunch alone or is excluded from peer groups, Doesn't know how to join games or activities already in progress, Misses social cues like facial expressions or tone of voice, Takes language literally (doesn't understand idioms, sarcasm, jokes).

mainEntity.name: How does a Social Stories™ and Social Narratives help with social communication difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Social Stories™, developed by Carol Gray, are short, personalized narratives that describe social situations, explain what typically happens, and provide guidance on appropriate responses. For children who struggle with the unwritten rules of school, Social Stories make those rules explicit. A story might explain what happens in the cafeteria, how to join a game at recess, or what to do when someone says 'hello.' The format uses specific sentence types: descriptive sentences (what happens), perspective sentences (what others think/feel), directive sentences (what the child can try), and affirmative sentences (values and rules). Social Stories work because they provide the information neurotypical children pick up implicitly through observation. They can be read repeatedly, allowing the child to prepare for situations before they occur. Stories can be customized for specific challenges the child faces — whether it's knowing when to raise their hand, understanding why kids laugh at certain jokes, or learning what 'being a good sport' means. They turn abstract social concepts into concrete, understandable narratives. Price: ₹500–2,000 (books); DIY free. DIY option: Creating and using Social Stories for school: 1) IDENTIFY THE SITUATION: pick ONE specific social challenge the child faces at school — be precise (not 'recess' but 'joining a game of tag at recess'), 2) GATHER INFORMATION: observe or ask about what typically happens in that situation — the sequence of events, unwritten expectations, common mistakes, 3) WRITE IN FIRST PERSON: 'When I want to join a game...' — child as the main character, 4) USE SENTENCE TYPES: Descriptive: 'At recess, kids play games on the playground.' Perspective: 'Kids usually like it when someone asks to join.' Directive: 'I can walk up and say, Can I play?' Affirmative: 'It's okay if they say the game has already started,' 5) RATIO MATTERS: 2-5 descriptive/perspective sentences for every 1 directive sentence — don't make it all instructions, 6) KEEP IT SHORT: younger children = shorter stories (5-10 sentences); older children can handle more complex stories, 7) ADD VISUALS: photographs or illustrations help, especially for younger children or visual learners, 8) READ REPEATEDLY: review the story before the situation occurs — morning before school, or right before recess, 9) PRACTICE: role-play the scenario after reading if possible, 10) UPDATE: revise stories as situations change or child masters the skill. COMMON SCHOOL TOPICS: joining games, conversation starters, taking turns talking, handling losing, asking for help, group project cooperation, cafeteria behavior, hallway behavior, responding to teasing. RESOURCES: Carol Gray's Social Stories website has guidelines and examples; books like 'The New Social Story Book' provide templates. Safety: Social Stories should describe, not prescribe. Avoid making them punitive or fear-based. They should build understanding, not compliance through fear.

mainEntity.name: How does a Comic Strip Conversations help with social communication difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Comic Strip Conversations, also developed by Carol Gray, use simple drawings to illustrate what people say, think, and feel during social interactions. For children who struggle to understand the multiple layers of social communication — that what people say isn't always what they mean, that different people have different thoughts simultaneously, that tone of voice changes meaning — comic strip conversations make these invisible elements visible. The technique uses stick figures, speech bubbles (what people SAY), thought bubbles (what people THINK), and color coding for emotions. After a social situation occurs (successful or unsuccessful), adult and child draw it together, exploring what each person was thinking and feeling. This builds perspective-taking skills and helps children understand where social interactions went wrong or right. Comic Strip Conversations are particularly effective for processing social conflicts or confusing situations. They slow down fast-moving social dynamics into analyzable frames. They reveal the hidden thoughts and feelings that the child missed in real time. They provide a visual record that can be reviewed and discussed. Over time, children internalize the habit of considering multiple perspectives. Price: ₹100–500 (materials); mostly DIY. DIY option: Creating Comic Strip Conversations: 1) MATERIALS NEEDED: paper, colored pencils/markers (specific colors for emotions — calm blue, angry red, happy yellow, sad green, etc.), 2) WHEN TO USE: after a social interaction the child found confusing, or to prepare for upcoming situations, 3) START WITH THE SCENE: draw simple stick figures representing the people involved — keep it simple, 4) SPEECH BUBBLES: 'What did they SAY?' — draw speech bubbles with the actual words spoken, 5) THOUGHT BUBBLES: 'What do you think they were THINKING?' — this is where the real learning happens; child may not know, so you can offer possibilities, 6) COLOR CODE: 'How was each person FEELING?' — use agreed-upon colors; anger might be red, confusion might be orange, happiness might be yellow, 7) MULTIPLE FRAMES: draw the interaction in sequence — what happened first, then what happened, then what happened after, 8) EXPLORE ALTERNATIVES: after drawing what happened, draw 'what could happen next time' — alternative endings, 9) CHILD CONTRIBUTES: child should draw or help draw as much as possible — this is collaborative, 10) KEEP THEM: save comic strip conversations to review patterns and progress. SCHOOL APPLICATIONS: processing recess conflicts, understanding why a classmate was upset, analyzing group work breakdowns, preparing for anticipated social challenges, debriefing after social successes (what made it work?). PERSPECTIVE PRACTICE: draw the SAME situation from different people's perspectives — 'What was happening in Rahul's mind? What about in your mind?' Safety: Focus on understanding, not blame. Comic Strip Conversations should help the child understand situations, not make them feel bad about mistakes. Keep the tone curious, not critical.

mainEntity.name: How does a Social Skills Games and Board Games help with social communication difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Board games and structured social skills games create a controlled, rule-governed environment where social interaction skills can be practiced. Unlike the chaotic, fast-moving social dynamics of the playground, games have explicit rules, clear turn-taking structures, defined beginnings and endings, and predictable sequences. This structure makes them ideal practice grounds for children developing social skills. Specific social skills games target skills directly — conversation topic cards, emotion recognition games, perspective-taking challenges. Traditional board games teach waiting, turn-taking, winning and losing gracefully, following rules, and cooperative or competitive play. The key is that games make practicing social skills FUN. Children are motivated to participate, and they get repeated practice without the pressure of real-world consequences. Mistakes in a game are just part of playing; mistakes on the playground carry social costs. Games can be used in therapy, in social skills groups, at home with family, and eventually with peers — bridging from structured practice to naturalistic application. Price: ₹800–3,500. DIY option: Using games for social skills development: 1) SKILL-SPECIFIC GAMES: 'The Social Skills Game' (child-specific skills), 'Q's Race to the Top' (social-emotional learning), 'The Talking, Feeling & Doing Game' (therapy game), conversation starter card games, emotion charades, 2) TRADITIONAL GAMES WITH SOCIAL LEARNING: Uno (turn-taking, rule-following, winning/losing), Candy Land (waiting, simple turn-taking for younger kids), Sorry! (handling luck and setback), Jenga (taking turns, handling tension), cooperative games like Forbidden Island (teamwork), 3) PRE-TEACH GAME SKILLS: before playing, discuss the rules, what winning and losing look like, what to say when you lose ('good game'), what NOT to say when you win (no gloating), 4) NARRATE SOCIAL ELEMENTS: 'It's Maya's turn — we wait. Now it's your turn. I lost that round, but that's okay — that's how games work,' 5) PRACTICE LOSING: intentionally play games where child won't always win; practice emotional regulation around losing, 6) MODEL GOOD SPORTSMANSHIP: adult demonstrates handling frustration, congratulating winner, 7) PROMPT IN THE MOMENT: 'What can you say when you're waiting?' 'How can you congratulate him?' 8) PROGRESS TO PEERS: once skills are solid with family, invite peers for structured game play, 9) DEBRIEF AFTER: 'What was hard? What went well? What will you remember for next time?' 10) CONNECT TO SCHOOL: 'This is just like taking turns in class discussion' — bridge game skills to school settings. COOPERATIVE GAMES ESPECIALLY VALUABLE: games where all players work together against the game teach teamwork without the social challenge of competing against peers. Safety: Monitor emotional regulation during games. If a child is becoming dysregulated around winning/losing, take a break. Build tolerance gradually. Some children may need to start with cooperative-only games.

mainEntity.name: How does a Emotion Recognition Cards and Faces help with social communication difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Reading facial expressions is fundamental to social interaction — knowing when someone is happy, angry, bored, or confused guides appropriate responses. Many children with social skills difficulties struggle with emotion recognition, either failing to notice expressions entirely or misinterpreting them. Emotion recognition cards provide systematic practice in identifying emotions from facial expressions, body language, and situational context. The cards typically show photographs or illustrations of faces displaying various emotions, ranging from basic (happy, sad, angry) to complex (embarrassed, anxious, disappointed). Activities include naming emotions, matching emotions to situations, identifying what might cause each emotion, and role-playing appropriate responses. For school success, this skill is essential: knowing when a classmate is upset (and should be comforted or given space), recognizing when a teacher is annoyed (and adjusting behavior), understanding when peers are bored (and changing topics or activities). Emotion recognition training has research support and is a foundational component of most social skills curricula. Price: ₹400–1,500. DIY option: Using emotion recognition materials: 1) START BASIC: begin with clear, distinct basic emotions — happy, sad, angry, scared, surprised; master these before adding complex emotions, 2) USE REAL PHOTOS: for real-world application, photo cards are often more effective than cartoon illustrations — they show real facial variation, 3) MULTIPLE ANGLES: show same emotion on different faces to build generalization — different ages, genders, ethnicities displaying same emotion, 4) INTENSITY VARIATIONS: show same emotion at different intensities — a little annoyed vs. very angry; this helps children read social situations more accurately, 5) NAME AND DESCRIBE: 'What emotion is this? How do you know? What does her face look like?' — build conscious awareness of cues (eyebrows, mouth, eyes), 6) MATCH TO SITUATIONS: 'When might someone feel this way?' — connect expressions to contexts, 7) PREDICT RESPONSES: 'If someone looks like this, what might they need? What could you do?' — build response repertoire, 8) PRACTICE IN CONTEXT: pause TV shows or videos to identify emotions; look at family photos and discuss expressions, 9) SELF-IDENTIFICATION: 'When do YOU feel this way? What does your face look like when you're frustrated?' — build self-awareness alongside other-awareness, 10) COMPLEX EMOTIONS: once basics are solid, add nuanced emotions — embarrassed, anxious, confused, disappointed, nervous, proud, bored, disgusted, surprised vs. shocked. SCHOOL APPLICATION: 'If your teacher's face looks like THIS, what do you think is happening? What should you do differently?' Safety: Some children may find intensive focus on faces uncomfortable. Respect pacing. Avoid forcing eye contact in the name of teaching emotion recognition.

mainEntity.name: How does a Conversation Cue Cards and Scripts help with social communication difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Conversation is one of the most complex social skills — it requires reading partner interest, taking turns, staying on topic, showing interest, asking follow-up questions, knowing when to talk and when to listen, understanding when a conversation is ending, and much more. For children who struggle with conversation, scripted cue cards provide essential scaffolding. Conversation starter cards give children something to say when they don't know how to begin. Topic cards provide subjects for discussion. Turn-taking visual cues remind children when to talk and when to listen. Conversation flow maps show the expected sequence of exchanges. Scripts for common situations (greeting classmates, joining lunch table, asking to play) reduce the cognitive load of generating language in the moment. The goal isn't to make conversations robotic — it's to provide scaffolding until conversation skills become more natural. Like training wheels on a bicycle, scripts and cues are reduced as skills develop. For school situations, having practiced scripts for common scenarios (what to say when entering the classroom, how to ask a clarifying question, what to say when a classmate is upset) gives children confidence and competence in moments that would otherwise be confusing. Price: ₹300–1,200. DIY option: Creating and using conversation supports: 1) CONVERSATION STARTERS: cards with openers — 'What did you do this weekend?' 'Have you seen [popular show]?' 'What's your favorite game?' — practice with cards at home, 2) TOPIC CARDS: when stuck, pull a topic card — 'movies,' 'sports,' 'games,' 'weekend plans' — provides something to talk about, 3) QUESTION PROMPTS: teach follow-up questions — 'That's cool. Where did you go?' 'What was the best part?' 'Do you do that often?' — keep conversation going, 4) TURN-TAKING VISUALS: simple visual showing 'My turn to talk' / 'My turn to listen' — can be a card passed back and forth, or a visual reminder the child keeps, 5) CONVERSATION RECIPE: visual showing typical conversation structure — Greeting → Topic → Questions → Responses → Closing — child can check where they are in the 'recipe', 6) SCRIPTS FOR COMMON SITUATIONS: write out what to say for specific school situations: entering classroom ('Good morning, Ms. [teacher]'), joining lunch table ('Is this seat taken?'), responding to 'What's up?' ('Not much, you?'), 7) PRACTICE EXTENSIVELY: scripts only work if practiced repeatedly; role-play the situations until responses are automatic, 8) POCKET CUES: small cards child can keep in pocket for reference — conversation starters, coping phrases, scripts, 9) TEACH READING INTEREST: 'If someone looks away, gives short answers, or starts doing something else, they might be done talking. You can say, I'll let you go!' 10) FADING SUPPORT: as skills develop, fade from scripts to topic prompts to occasional cues to independent conversation. SCHOOL-SPECIFIC SCRIPTS: 'Can I borrow a pencil?' 'I don't understand — can you explain?' 'Want to be partners?' 'Can I play too?' 'Good game' (after losing). Safety: Scripts should sound natural for the child's age and context. Overly formal or unusual scripts may make the child stand out. Listen to how peers actually talk and match that register.

mainEntity.name: How does a Social Thinking® Curriculum Materials (Zones of Regulation, Superflex, etc.) help with social communication difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Social Thinking® is a research-informed methodology developed by Michelle Garcia Winner that teaches the cognitive processes underlying social interaction. Rather than teaching behaviors in isolation ('say please'), it teaches the thinking that leads to social success ('think about what others are thinking and feeling'). Materials from Social Thinking include: The Zones of Regulation (four color-coded zones representing emotional/energy states — blue for low energy/sad, green for ready to learn/calm, yellow for elevated/anxious, red for high alert/angry); Superflex and the Team of Unthinkables (superhero curriculum teaching flexible thinking); The Incredible Flexible You (early childhood curriculum); Thinking About You Thinking About Me (perspective taking). These materials provide a shared vocabulary for discussing social thinking. Instead of saying 'stop being weird,' a teacher can say 'that's a yellow zone thought' or 'Rock Brain is making you stuck.' The concepts are visual, memorable, and applicable across settings. For school, the Zones of Regulation is particularly valuable because it gives children a way to identify and communicate their emotional state, and links zones to expected behaviors and regulation strategies. Price: ₹1,500–5,000. DIY option: Implementing Social Thinking concepts: 1) ZONES OF REGULATION: teach the four zones using the curriculum or adapted materials — Blue (low energy, tired, sad), Green (calm, focused, ready), Yellow (heightened, anxious, silly, wiggly), Red (extremely heightened, angry, terrified, out of control); identify current zone throughout the day; teach strategies for getting to/staying in green, 2) SIZE OF THE PROBLEM: teach that problems come in different sizes and reactions should match — small problem (someone cuts in line) = small reaction (ask politely); big problem (emergency) = big reaction (get adult immediately), 3) EXPECTED vs. UNEXPECTED: teach that some behaviors are 'expected' (what others expect in a situation) and some are 'unexpected' (surprises others, may make them uncomfortable); practice identifying expected/unexpected in various scenarios, 4) SUPERFLEX: use the superhero character and Team of Unthinkables to personify flexible vs. rigid thinking; identify when 'Rock Brain' (stuck thinking), 'Brain Eater' (not thinking about others), or other Unthinkables are active, 5) THINKING WITH YOUR EYES: teach that we gather social information by looking at people (what they're doing, how they look, what they might be thinking), 6) BODY IN THE GROUP: teach expected body positioning for group participation — facing the group, following the action, being in the appropriate space, 7) WHOLE BODY LISTENING: more than just ears — eyes looking, ears listening, mouth quiet, body still, brain thinking, heart caring, 8) SOCIAL DETECTIVE: teach children to observe and gather clues about social situations before acting, 9) USE ACROSS SETTINGS: vocabulary should be used at home, school, and therapy — consistent language across environments, 10) CONNECT TO REAL SITUATIONS: 'What zone are you in right now?' 'Was that expected or unexpected?' 'What size problem is this?' Apply concepts to daily life. Safety: Social Thinking concepts should teach understanding, not shame. 'Unexpected' behaviors aren't necessarily wrong — they just surprise others. Focus on social understanding, not compliance.

mainEntity.name: What are the warning signs that need professional help?

acceptedAnswer.text: REAL WORLD EVIDENCE from Pinnacle Blooms Network: 20M+ Exclusive 1:1 Sessions. 97%+ Measured Improvement. 70+ Centers.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Social Communication Difficulties
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Social skills are the complex set of learned behaviors that allow us to interact effectively with others. For neurotypical children, many social skills are acquired implicitly through observation, imitation, and social feedback. For children with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, social communication disorder, anxiety, or other conditions, these skills often require explicit, systematic teaching. School is a particularly demanding social environment because it requires navigating multiple contexts (classroom, cafeteria, playground, hallways), multiple relationship types (peers, teachers, staff), and complex unwritten rules that vary by setting and age. Children who struggle socially at school face significant consequences: isolation, rejection, bullying, anxiety, depression, declining academic performance, and damaged self-esteem. Early and ongoing social skills intervention can dramatically improve outcomes. The most effective approaches combine explicit teaching of skills, opportunities for practice in structured settings, and support for generalization to natural environments. Materials that make invisible social rules visible (Social Stories, Comic Strip Conversations), provide practice opportunities (games, structured activities), and support regulation (fidgets, Zones of Regulation) form the foundation of effective social skills work. The goal is not to change who the child is, but to give them tools to participate in the social world in ways that feel authentic and rewarding to them.

supply.name: Social Skills Games and Board Games

step.name: Understand the Problem
step.text: Social skills at school encompass the complex set of abilities required to successfully navigate peer interactions, classroom dynamics, and the unwritten social rules of educational settings. These skills include: initiating and maintaining conversations, joining ongoing activities appropriately, taking turns, cooperating in groups, reading social cues (facial expressions, body language, tone of voice), understanding perspective of others, recognizing and responding to emotions, using appropriate humor, resolving conflicts, and following the implicit social hierarchy and rules of the school environment. For neurotypical children, many of these skills develop naturally through observation and implicit learning. For children with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, social communication disorder, anxiety, or other conditions, these skills often need to be explicitly taught, practiced, and supported with visual aids and structured approaches. School presents unique social challenges: the playground is unstructured and fast-moving, the cafeteria requires navigating seating politics, group work demands collaboration skills, and classroom participation requires understanding when and how to speak. Children who struggle socially at school often experience isolation, bullying, anxiety, and declining self-esteem — even when their academic performance is strong. Social skills intervention focuses on making the invisible rules visible, providing practice opportunities, and building the cognitive frameworks for understanding social situations. Social skills are the complex set of learned behaviors that allow us to interact effectively with others. For neurotypical children, many social skills are acquired implicitly through observation, imitation, and social feedback. For children with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, social communication disorder, anxiety, or other conditions, these skills often require explicit, systematic teaching. School is a particularly demanding social environment because it requires navigating multiple contexts (classroom, cafeteria, playground, hallways), multiple relationship types (peers, teachers, staff), and complex unwritten rules that vary by setting and age. Children who struggle socially at school face significant consequences: isolation, rejection, bullying, anxiety, depression, declining academic performance, and damaged self-esteem. Early and ongoing social skills intervention can dramatically improve outcomes. The most effective approaches combine explicit teaching of skills, opportunities for practice in structured settings, and support for generalization to natural environments. Materials that make invisible social rules visible (Social Stories, Comic Strip Conversations), provide practice opportunities (games, structured activities), and support regulation (fidgets, Zones of Regulation) form the foundation of effective social skills work. The goal is not to change who the child is, but to give them tools to participate in the social world in ways that feel authentic and rewarding to them.

step.name: Start with Foundation Materials
step.text: Begin with: Social Stories™ and Social Narratives, Comic Strip Conversations, Social Skills Games and Board Games.

signOrSymptom.name: Has difficulty making or keeping friends at school
Eats lunch alone or is excluded from peer groups
Doesn't know how to join games or activities already in progress
Misses social cues like facial expressions or tone of voice
Takes language literally (doesn't understand idioms, sarcasm, jokes)
Talks excessively about own interests without noticing listener disinterest
Struggles with group work — either dominates or withdraws

possibleTreatment.name: Sensory Integration Therapy
possibleTreatment.description: Occupational therapy using graded sensory input

@graph.name: 9 Materials for Social Communication Difficulties
@graph.description: Evidence-based materials for ages 5–14 years (school-age, with materials adapted for developmental and grade level)

item.name: Social Stories™ and Social Narratives
item.description: Social Stories™, developed by Carol Gray, are short, personalized narratives that describe social situations, explain what typically happens, and provide guidance on appropriate responses. For children who struggle with the unwritten rules of school, Social Stories make those rules explicit. A story might explain what happens in the cafeteria, how to join a game at recess, or what to do when someone says 'hello.' The format uses specific sentence types: descriptive sentences (what happens), perspective sentences (what others think/feel), directive sentences (what the child can try), and affirmative sentences (values and rules). Social Stories work because they provide the information neurotypical children pick up implicitly through observation. They can be read repeatedly, allowing the child to prepare for situations before they occur. Stories can be customized for specific challenges the child faces — whether it's knowing when to raise their hand, understanding why kids laugh at certain jokes, or learning what 'being a good sport' means. They turn abstract social concepts into concrete, understandable narratives.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating and using Social Stories for school: 1) IDENTIFY THE SITUATION: pick ONE specific social challenge the child faces at school — be precise (not 'recess' but 'joining a game of tag at recess'), 2) GATHER INFORMATION: observe or ask about what typically happens in that situation — the sequence of events, unwritten expectations, common mistakes, 3) WRITE IN FIRST PERSON: 'When I want to join a game...' — child as the main character, 4) USE SENTENCE TYPES: Descriptive: 'At recess, kids play games on the playground.' Perspective: 'Kids usually like it when someone asks to join.' Directive: 'I can walk up and say, Can I play?' Affirmative: 'It's okay if they say the game has already started,' 5) RATIO MATTERS: 2-5 descriptive/perspective sentences for every 1 directive sentence — don't make it all instructions, 6) KEEP IT SHORT: younger children = shorter stories (5-10 sentences); older children can handle more complex stories, 7) ADD VISUALS: photographs or illustrations help, especially for younger children or visual learners, 8) READ REPEATEDLY: review the story before the situation occurs — morning before school, or right before recess, 9) PRACTICE: role-play the scenario after reading if possible, 10) UPDATE: revise stories as situations change or child masters the skill. COMMON SCHOOL TOPICS: joining games, conversation starters, taking turns talking, handling losing, asking for help, group project cooperation, cafeteria behavior, hallway behavior, responding to teasing. RESOURCES: Carol Gray's Social Stories website has guidelines and examples; books like 'The New Social Story Book' provide templates.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Social Stories should describe, not prescribe. Avoid making them punitive or fear-based. They should build understanding, not compliance through fear.

item.name: Comic Strip Conversations
item.description: Comic Strip Conversations, also developed by Carol Gray, use simple drawings to illustrate what people say, think, and feel during social interactions. For children who struggle to understand the multiple layers of social communication — that what people say isn't always what they mean, that different people have different thoughts simultaneously, that tone of voice changes meaning — comic strip conversations make these invisible elements visible. The technique uses stick figures, speech bubbles (what people SAY), thought bubbles (what people THINK), and color coding for emotions. After a social situation occurs (successful or unsuccessful), adult and child draw it together, exploring what each person was thinking and feeling. This builds perspective-taking skills and helps children understand where social interactions went wrong or right. Comic Strip Conversations are particularly effective for processing social conflicts or confusing situations. They slow down fast-moving social dynamics into analyzable frames. They reveal the hidden thoughts and feelings that the child missed in real time. They provide a visual record that can be reviewed and discussed. Over time, children internalize the habit of considering multiple perspectives.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating Comic Strip Conversations: 1) MATERIALS NEEDED: paper, colored pencils/markers (specific colors for emotions — calm blue, angry red, happy yellow, sad green, etc.), 2) WHEN TO USE: after a social interaction the child found confusing, or to prepare for upcoming situations, 3) START WITH THE SCENE: draw simple stick figures representing the people involved — keep it simple, 4) SPEECH BUBBLES: 'What did they SAY?' — draw speech bubbles with the actual words spoken, 5) THOUGHT BUBBLES: 'What do you think they were THINKING?' — this is where the real learning happens; child may not know, so you can offer possibilities, 6) COLOR CODE: 'How was each person FEELING?' — use agreed-upon colors; anger might be red, confusion might be orange, happiness might be yellow, 7) MULTIPLE FRAMES: draw the interaction in sequence — what happened first, then what happened, then what happened after, 8) EXPLORE ALTERNATIVES: after drawing what happened, draw 'what could happen next time' — alternative endings, 9) CHILD CONTRIBUTES: child should draw or help draw as much as possible — this is collaborative, 10) KEEP THEM: save comic strip conversations to review patterns and progress. SCHOOL APPLICATIONS: processing recess conflicts, understanding why a classmate was upset, analyzing group work breakdowns, preparing for anticipated social challenges, debriefing after social successes (what made it work?). PERSPECTIVE PRACTICE: draw the SAME situation from different people's perspectives — 'What was happening in Rahul's mind? What about in your mind?'

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Focus on understanding, not blame. Comic Strip Conversations should help the child understand situations, not make them feel bad about mistakes. Keep the tone curious, not critical.

item.name: Social Skills Games and Board Games
item.description: Board games and structured social skills games create a controlled, rule-governed environment where social interaction skills can be practiced. Unlike the chaotic, fast-moving social dynamics of the playground, games have explicit rules, clear turn-taking structures, defined beginnings and endings, and predictable sequences. This structure makes them ideal practice grounds for children developing social skills. Specific social skills games target skills directly — conversation topic cards, emotion recognition games, perspective-taking challenges. Traditional board games teach waiting, turn-taking, winning and losing gracefully, following rules, and cooperative or competitive play. The key is that games make practicing social skills FUN. Children are motivated to participate, and they get repeated practice without the pressure of real-world consequences. Mistakes in a game are just part of playing; mistakes on the playground carry social costs. Games can be used in therapy, in social skills groups, at home with family, and eventually with peers — bridging from structured practice to naturalistic application.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Using games for social skills development: 1) SKILL-SPECIFIC GAMES: 'The Social Skills Game' (child-specific skills), 'Q's Race to the Top' (social-emotional learning), 'The Talking, Feeling & Doing Game' (therapy game), conversation starter card games, emotion charades, 2) TRADITIONAL GAMES WITH SOCIAL LEARNING: Uno (turn-taking, rule-following, winning/losing), Candy Land (waiting, simple turn-taking for younger kids), Sorry! (handling luck and setback), Jenga (taking turns, handling tension), cooperative games like Forbidden Island (teamwork), 3) PRE-TEACH GAME SKILLS: before playing, discuss the rules, what winning and losing look like, what to say when you lose ('good game'), what NOT to say when you win (no gloating), 4) NARRATE SOCIAL ELEMENTS: 'It's Maya's turn — we wait. Now it's your turn. I lost that round, but that's okay — that's how games work,' 5) PRACTICE LOSING: intentionally play games where child won't always win; practice emotional regulation around losing, 6) MODEL GOOD SPORTSMANSHIP: adult demonstrates handling frustration, congratulating winner, 7) PROMPT IN THE MOMENT: 'What can you say when you're waiting?' 'How can you congratulate him?' 8) PROGRESS TO PEERS: once skills are solid with family, invite peers for structured game play, 9) DEBRIEF AFTER: 'What was hard? What went well? What will you remember for next time?' 10) CONNECT TO SCHOOL: 'This is just like taking turns in class discussion' — bridge game skills to school settings. COOPERATIVE GAMES ESPECIALLY VALUABLE: games where all players work together against the game teach teamwork without the social challenge of competing against peers.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Monitor emotional regulation during games. If a child is becoming dysregulated around winning/losing, take a break. Build tolerance gradually. Some children may need to start with cooperative-only games.

item.name: Emotion Recognition Cards and Faces
item.description: Reading facial expressions is fundamental to social interaction — knowing when someone is happy, angry, bored, or confused guides appropriate responses. Many children with social skills difficulties struggle with emotion recognition, either failing to notice expressions entirely or misinterpreting them. Emotion recognition cards provide systematic practice in identifying emotions from facial expressions, body language, and situational context. The cards typically show photographs or illustrations of faces displaying various emotions, ranging from basic (happy, sad, angry) to complex (embarrassed, anxious, disappointed). Activities include naming emotions, matching emotions to situations, identifying what might cause each emotion, and role-playing appropriate responses. For school success, this skill is essential: knowing when a classmate is upset (and should be comforted or given space), recognizing when a teacher is annoyed (and adjusting behavior), understanding when peers are bored (and changing topics or activities). Emotion recognition training has research support and is a foundational component of most social skills curricula.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Using emotion recognition materials: 1) START BASIC: begin with clear, distinct basic emotions — happy, sad, angry, scared, surprised; master these before adding complex emotions, 2) USE REAL PHOTOS: for real-world application, photo cards are often more effective than cartoon illustrations — they show real facial variation, 3) MULTIPLE ANGLES: show same emotion on different faces to build generalization — different ages, genders, ethnicities displaying same emotion, 4) INTENSITY VARIATIONS: show same emotion at different intensities — a little annoyed vs. very angry; this helps children read social situations more accurately, 5) NAME AND DESCRIBE: 'What emotion is this? How do you know? What does her face look like?' — build conscious awareness of cues (eyebrows, mouth, eyes), 6) MATCH TO SITUATIONS: 'When might someone feel this way?' — connect expressions to contexts, 7) PREDICT RESPONSES: 'If someone looks like this, what might they need? What could you do?' — build response repertoire, 8) PRACTICE IN CONTEXT: pause TV shows or videos to identify emotions; look at family photos and discuss expressions, 9) SELF-IDENTIFICATION: 'When do YOU feel this way? What does your face look like when you're frustrated?' — build self-awareness alongside other-awareness, 10) COMPLEX EMOTIONS: once basics are solid, add nuanced emotions — embarrassed, anxious, confused, disappointed, nervous, proud, bored, disgusted, surprised vs. shocked. SCHOOL APPLICATION: 'If your teacher's face looks like THIS, what do you think is happening? What should you do differently?'

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Some children may find intensive focus on faces uncomfortable. Respect pacing. Avoid forcing eye contact in the name of teaching emotion recognition.

item.name: Conversation Cue Cards and Scripts
item.description: Conversation is one of the most complex social skills — it requires reading partner interest, taking turns, staying on topic, showing interest, asking follow-up questions, knowing when to talk and when to listen, understanding when a conversation is ending, and much more. For children who struggle with conversation, scripted cue cards provide essential scaffolding. Conversation starter cards give children something to say when they don't know how to begin. Topic cards provide subjects for discussion. Turn-taking visual cues remind children when to talk and when to listen. Conversation flow maps show the expected sequence of exchanges. Scripts for common situations (greeting classmates, joining lunch table, asking to play) reduce the cognitive load of generating language in the moment. The goal isn't to make conversations robotic — it's to provide scaffolding until conversation skills become more natural. Like training wheels on a bicycle, scripts and cues are reduced as skills develop. For school situations, having practiced scripts for common scenarios (what to say when entering the classroom, how to ask a clarifying question, what to say when a classmate is upset) gives children confidence and competence in moments that would otherwise be confusing.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating and using conversation supports: 1) CONVERSATION STARTERS: cards with openers — 'What did you do this weekend?' 'Have you seen [popular show]?' 'What's your favorite game?' — practice with cards at home, 2) TOPIC CARDS: when stuck, pull a topic card — 'movies,' 'sports,' 'games,' 'weekend plans' — provides something to talk about, 3) QUESTION PROMPTS: teach follow-up questions — 'That's cool. Where did you go?' 'What was the best part?' 'Do you do that often?' — keep conversation going, 4) TURN-TAKING VISUALS: simple visual showing 'My turn to talk' / 'My turn to listen' — can be a card passed back and forth, or a visual reminder the child keeps, 5) CONVERSATION RECIPE: visual showing typical conversation structure — Greeting → Topic → Questions → Responses → Closing — child can check where they are in the 'recipe', 6) SCRIPTS FOR COMMON SITUATIONS: write out what to say for specific school situations: entering classroom ('Good morning, Ms. [teacher]'), joining lunch table ('Is this seat taken?'), responding to 'What's up?' ('Not much, you?'), 7) PRACTICE EXTENSIVELY: scripts only work if practiced repeatedly; role-play the situations until responses are automatic, 8) POCKET CUES: small cards child can keep in pocket for reference — conversation starters, coping phrases, scripts, 9) TEACH READING INTEREST: 'If someone looks away, gives short answers, or starts doing something else, they might be done talking. You can say, I'll let you go!' 10) FADING SUPPORT: as skills develop, fade from scripts to topic prompts to occasional cues to independent conversation. SCHOOL-SPECIFIC SCRIPTS: 'Can I borrow a pencil?' 'I don't understand — can you explain?' 'Want to be partners?' 'Can I play too?' 'Good game' (after losing).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Scripts should sound natural for the child's age and context. Overly formal or unusual scripts may make the child stand out. Listen to how peers actually talk and match that register.

item.name: Social Thinking® Curriculum Materials (Zones of Regulation, Superflex, etc.)
item.description: Social Thinking® is a research-informed methodology developed by Michelle Garcia Winner that teaches the cognitive processes underlying social interaction. Rather than teaching behaviors in isolation ('say please'), it teaches the thinking that leads to social success ('think about what others are thinking and feeling'). Materials from Social Thinking include: The Zones of Regulation (four color-coded zones representing emotional/energy states — blue for low energy/sad, green for ready to learn/calm, yellow for elevated/anxious, red for high alert/angry); Superflex and the Team of Unthinkables (superhero curriculum teaching flexible thinking); The Incredible Flexible You (early childhood curriculum); Thinking About You Thinking About Me (perspective taking). These materials provide a shared vocabulary for discussing social thinking. Instead of saying 'stop being weird,' a teacher can say 'that's a yellow zone thought' or 'Rock Brain is making you stuck.' The concepts are visual, memorable, and applicable across settings. For school, the Zones of Regulation is particularly valuable because it gives children a way to identify and communicate their emotional state, and links zones to expected behaviors and regulation strategies.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Implementing Social Thinking concepts: 1) ZONES OF REGULATION: teach the four zones using the curriculum or adapted materials — Blue (low energy, tired, sad), Green (calm, focused, ready), Yellow (heightened, anxious, silly, wiggly), Red (extremely heightened, angry, terrified, out of control); identify current zone throughout the day; teach strategies for getting to/staying in green, 2) SIZE OF THE PROBLEM: teach that problems come in different sizes and reactions should match — small problem (someone cuts in line) = small reaction (ask politely); big problem (emergency) = big reaction (get adult immediately), 3) EXPECTED vs. UNEXPECTED: teach that some behaviors are 'expected' (what others expect in a situation) and some are 'unexpected' (surprises others, may make them uncomfortable); practice identifying expected/unexpected in various scenarios, 4) SUPERFLEX: use the superhero character and Team of Unthinkables to personify flexible vs. rigid thinking; identify when 'Rock Brain' (stuck thinking), 'Brain Eater' (not thinking about others), or other Unthinkables are active, 5) THINKING WITH YOUR EYES: teach that we gather social information by looking at people (what they're doing, how they look, what they might be thinking), 6) BODY IN THE GROUP: teach expected body positioning for group participation — facing the group, following the action, being in the appropriate space, 7) WHOLE BODY LISTENING: more than just ears — eyes looking, ears listening, mouth quiet, body still, brain thinking, heart caring, 8) SOCIAL DETECTIVE: teach children to observe and gather clues about social situations before acting, 9) USE ACROSS SETTINGS: vocabulary should be used at home, school, and therapy — consistent language across environments, 10) CONNECT TO REAL SITUATIONS: 'What zone are you in right now?' 'Was that expected or unexpected?' 'What size problem is this?' Apply concepts to daily life.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Social Thinking concepts should teach understanding, not shame. 'Unexpected' behaviors aren't necessarily wrong — they just surprise others. Focus on social understanding, not compliance.

item.name: Video Modeling and Social Skills Videos
item.description: Video modeling uses the power of visual learning to teach social skills by showing correct behavior in action. For many children, seeing is understanding. A video showing children appropriately joining a game, handling a disagreement, or making conversation provides a model that can be watched, rewound, discussed, and imitated. Video modeling works because: (1) it's visual and engaging, (2) it can be watched multiple times for learning and reinforcement, (3) it slows down fast-moving social interactions into observable sequences, (4) it shows the desired outcome clearly, and (5) many children are highly motivated by video content. There are two main types: commercial social skills videos (Model Me Kids, etc.) that show actors demonstrating skills, and self-modeling where the child is recorded successfully performing skills. Self-modeling is particularly powerful — seeing yourself doing something correctly builds confidence and provides a memorable model. For school skills specifically, videos showing classroom behavior, cafeteria skills, playground interaction, and peer conversation provide directly applicable models. Videos can be used to prepare for situations, to debrief after challenges, and to maintain learned skills.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Using video modeling for social skills: 1) COMMERCIAL OPTIONS: Model Me Kids (videos for autism), Social Skills TV, Social Express, Everyday Speech — professionally produced social skills videos covering various scenarios, 2) SKILL-SPECIFIC SELECTION: choose videos that match the specific skill your child needs — starting conversations, handling bullying, group work, etc., 3) WATCH TOGETHER: don't just show videos — watch together and discuss: 'What did she do first?' 'Why did he say that?' 'What happened because of that?', 4) PAUSE AND PREDICT: pause videos and ask 'What do you think will happen next?' — builds social prediction skills, 5) REPEAT VIEWING: children benefit from watching the same videos multiple times; repetition builds learning, 6) CONNECT TO REAL LIFE: 'Remember how the boy in the video asked to join? You can try that at recess tomorrow,' 7) SELF-MODELING: record your child successfully performing social skills during practice sessions; watching themselves doing it correctly is powerful, 8) VIDEO ANALYSIS: occasionally record real social interactions (with permission) to analyze later — slow down and discuss what went well, what was tricky, 9) CREATE YOUR OWN: for specific school situations, create custom videos — parent or sibling can model the skill in context, 10) YOUTUBE CAUTION: social skills content on YouTube varies widely in quality; preview before showing to child. SCHOOL COORDINATION: share videos with teachers and school staff so they can use the same models and language.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Preview all videos before showing to child to ensure appropriateness. Monitor screen time and balance with other forms of learning and practice.

item.name: Fidgets and Self-Regulation Tools
item.description: Social success at school requires emotional and sensory regulation. A child who is dysregulated — anxious, overwhelmed, overstimulated, or under-aroused — cannot effectively read social cues, control impulses, or navigate complex interactions. Fidgets and self-regulation tools help children achieve and maintain the regulated state necessary for social engagement. Fidget tools provide sensory input that helps some children focus, stay calm, and regulate arousal levels. Stress balls, fidget cubes, putty, textured items, and similar tools give the sensory-seeking child appropriate input without disruptive behavior. For social situations specifically, regulation tools can: reduce anxiety before social interactions, provide calming input during overwhelming social situations (like the cafeteria), help maintain focus during conversations and group work, and give an alternative to potentially disruptive stimming behaviors. The key is matching the tool to the child's sensory needs and the social context — a discreet fidget that can be used under a desk is different from a loud or visually distracting item. Self-regulation tools also include visual supports like calm-down cards, regulation strategy menus, and Zones check-in materials.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating a self-regulation toolkit for school social success: 1) IDENTIFY NEEDS: does child seek or avoid sensory input? Are they under-aroused (need alerting input) or over-aroused (need calming input)? What helps them regulate? 2) SCHOOL-APPROPRIATE TOOLS: fidgets for school must be discreet (not distracting to others), quiet (no clicking or noise), and appropriate (approved by teacher); fidget cubes, therapy putty, stress balls, textured fabric swatches, 3) MATCH TO SITUATION: different tools for different moments — calming tools before anxiety-provoking social situations, alerting tools when under-aroused and disengaged, 4) PORTABLE KIT: small pouch with regulation tools child can keep in desk or backpack; might include fidget, calm-down cards, visual supports, 5) TEACH USE: explicitly teach when and how to use tools — 'Before recess, if you feel nervous, you can squeeze your stress ball three times,' 6) PRACTICE REGULATION STRATEGIES: tools are only part of regulation; also teach deep breathing, counting, taking space, asking for help, 7) ZONES CHECK-IN: if using Zones of Regulation, include a zones check-in card — child identifies current zone and what might help, 8) CALM-DOWN PROTOCOL: teach what to do when dysregulated — 'I notice I'm in yellow zone. I can take three deep breaths, use my fidget, or ask for a break,' 9) TEACHER COORDINATION: discuss with teacher so regulation tools are permitted and supported; teacher can cue child to use tools, 10) GENERALIZE TO SOCIAL: connect regulation to social outcomes — 'When you're in the green zone, it's easier to talk to friends. Let's make sure you're regulated before lunch.' CAUTION: fidgets should help, not become a distraction or obsession. Monitor whether the tool is serving its purpose.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Choose fidgets appropriate for child's age (no small parts for younger children). Ensure fidgets are school-approved. Monitor for appropriate use versus distraction or disruption.

item.name: Structured Peer Interaction Activities and Games
item.description: The ultimate goal of social skills work is successful interaction with PEERS in natural settings. But unstructured peer interaction (like recess) can be overwhelming for children still developing social skills. Structured peer activities provide a bridge — the opportunity to interact with peers within a framework that increases the chance of success. Structured activities include: organized games with clear rules, cooperative activities with defined roles, facilitated social groups, buddy programs, shared interest clubs, and partner activities during class. The structure reduces the cognitive and social demand by providing clear expectations, defined turns, explicit rules, and adult support as needed. For school, structured peer activities might include: lunch bunch groups (small, facilitated lunch groups), recess clubs (organized activities during recess), cooperative learning structures in class, peer buddy systems, and special interest clubs. The key is that practice happens WITH actual peers (not just adults) but WITH enough structure to ensure success. Over time, structure can be gradually reduced as skills develop and confidence grows.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating and accessing structured peer activities: 1) LUNCH BUNCH: ask school about small, facilitated lunch groups where children eat with a counselor or speech therapist and practice conversation skills; if not available, advocate for this, 2) RECESS CLUBS: request or help organize structured recess options — board game club, reading club, organized sport — for children who struggle with unstructured recess, 3) COOPERATIVE LEARNING: advocate for classroom use of cooperative learning structures where group roles are defined and everyone has a job, 4) BUDDY SYSTEMS: peer buddy programs pair children with social challenges with socially skilled peers for support during transitions or unstructured times, 5) INTEREST-BASED CLUBS: help child join clubs based on genuine interests — if they love chess, join chess club; shared interest provides connection foundation, 6) HOME PLAYDATES — STRUCTURED: when arranging playdates, have activities planned — specific game, specific craft, specific video to watch together; don't expect unstructured 'play,' 7) ONE PEER AT A TIME: start with one-on-one interaction before groups; dyad is easier than managing multiple social relationships simultaneously, 8) FACILITATE BUT FADE: adult starts as active facilitator, then gradually steps back as children become more independent in interactions, 9) CHOOSE PEERS CAREFULLY: for early practice, seek peers who are patient, kind, and share interests with your child; success breeds success, 10) DEBRIEF AFTER: after peer activities, discuss what went well and what was challenging — build awareness and learning. SCHOOL ADVOCACY: if your child's school lacks structured social options, advocate for them. Present the research on structured social skills support. Offer to help organize. Connect with the school counselor.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Monitor peer interactions for bullying or exclusion. Structure should protect, not expose. If peer activities are unsuccessful, increase support or adjust groupings rather than blaming the child.

socialMedia.caption: When the school's social rules are invisible to your child — and the playground feels like a foreign country. School isn't just academics. It's a complex social world with unwritten rules that some children can't decode. Here are 9 materials that help: Social Stories™, Comic Strip Conversations, social skills games, emotion recognition cards, conversation cue cards, Social Thinking® curriculum, video modeling, fidgets and regulation tools, and structured peer activities. From isolation to connection. From confusion to understanding. From watching from the sidelines to playing the game. 💙 #SocialSkills #SchoolSuccess #AutismSupport #PinnacleBlooms
